title: A Theoretical Investigation of the Thermal and Photochemical Mechanisms of Ethylbenzene Dehydrogenation on Rutile TiO$_{2}$(110)
abstract: This master's thesis investigates the thermal and photochemical dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene (EB) to styrene on the rutile TiO$_{2}$(110) surface. A dual-methodological quantum chemical approach is used for this investigation. While industrial styrene production is energy-intensive, photocatalysis on semiconductor materials offers a promising alternative under significantly milder conditions. To elucidate the underlying mechanisms, this study employs density functional theory (DFT-PBE-D3) for geometry optimization and high-level multi-reference methods (SA-CASSCF) to accurately describe the electronic complexity of excited states and radical intermediates.   The investigation reveals that, on the stoichiometric surface, both thermal and photochemical pathways are dominated by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET). The wavelength dependence observed in the literature is explained by how the system navigates electronic manifolds. 343 nm irradiation leads to rapid relaxation into the ground state, where high kinetic barriers persist. In contrast, 257 nm excitation enables the system to persist in higher excited states (S1/T2). This allows the reaction to bypass the rate-determining ground-state barrier.   Furthermore, the study demonstrates that surface oxidation causes a fundamental mechanistic shift. On oxidized surfaces, pre-adsorbed oxygen radicals (O$_{Ti}$) enable direct hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), which is more efficient than PCET on reduced surfaces. This "hydrogen scavenger" effect explains the significant increase in styrene yield. This work underscores the necessity of multi-reference treatments for complex surface reactions and provides a fundamental understanding of how surface stoichiometry and photon energy govern photocatalytic efficiency.
summary: This thesis explores how light and surface chemistry can be used to create a more sustainable, low-temperature alternative to the energy-intensive industrial production of styrene. By using advanced quantum chemical simulations, the author demonstrates that specific UV wavelengths and surface oxidation can bypass high energy barriers through excited-state mechanisms. The research highlights the importance of multi-reference methods in accurately modeling complex surface-catalyzed reactions involving radical intermediates.
main_problem: Investigating the thermal and photochemical mechanisms of ethylbenzene dehydrogenation to styrene on rutile TiO2(110) surfaces, specifically focusing on the role of surface oxidation and photon energy.
main_result: The study finds that thermal pathways are dominated by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET), while higher energy photons (257 nm) enable a more efficient pathway by accessing higher excited states to bypass ground-state barriers. Additionally, surface oxidation introduces a more efficient hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism via oxygen radicals.
method: A dual-methodological quantum chemical approach using Density Functional Theory (DFT-P0BE-D3) for geometry optimization and high-level multi-reference methods (SA-CASSCF/NEVPT2) to describe excited states and radical intermediates.
model_or_system: An embedded cluster model representing the rutile TiO2(110) surface, including both stoichiometric and oxidized (with pre-adsorbed oxygen radicals) configurations, with ethylbenzene as the adsorbate.
key_observables: Activation energies (Ea), adsorption energies, electronic roots (S0, S1, T2, etc.), spin density, and reaction coordinates (bond lengths).
important_parameters: Wavelengths of 343 nm and 257 nm; temperature regimes (thermal vs. photocatalytic); surface oxidation state.
main_assumptions: The study uses a cluster model to represent the infinite surface and acknowledges that a complete mapping of the multidimensional potential energy surface was not possible due to computational complexity.
figures_summary: Figures include the industrial styrene synthesis equation, structural models of the TiO2 surface and clusters, mechanistic schematics for photocatalysis, and energy profiles comparing thermal and photochemical pathways across different electronic states.
paper_structure: The work begins with industrial context, followed by a description of the computational models and methods, detailed analysis of adsorption and thermal mechanisms, investigation of photochemical pathways and wavelength dependence, an exploration of the effects of surface oxidation, and a concluding discussion on future research directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The chemical equation of the industrial synthesis of styrene from ethylbenzene.
Figure 2. Representation of rutile(110) bulk. The blue atoms represent titanium, and the red atoms symbolize oxygen. The orange-circled atoms are the relevant oxygen and titanium species.16,19

Figure 3. Schematic representation of the underlying step of a photochemical reaction. In this step, a photon (h · ν) excites the semiconductor material, transferring an electron (e−) to the conduction band (CB) and leaving a hole (h+) in the valence band (VB).16,19
Figure 4. A proposed photocatalytic reaction mechanism for the dehydrogenation of ethylben- zene to styrene. This mechanism was created using information from source4,6,7,9,18.
Figure 5. Jacob’s ladder illustrates the various functionals (LDA, GGA and Hybrid GGA) that leading to enhanced accuracy in calculations. The image is adapted from34,35.
Figure 6. Visualization of the inactive, external and active orbital spaces in a CASSCF calcu- lation.29

Figure 7. a) All four Regions of the Embedded cluster model are shown. b) Cluster embedded in a half-spherical field of point charges.10 c) All atoms with and without charge that are treated in all calculations. (Color scheme: red – oxygen, blue – titanium, orange – effective core potential (boundary region 2), violet - effective core potential (boundary region 1), green/see-through - point charge field)10–12
Figure 8. Frontier molecular orbitals of the physisorbed singlet state (RKS-PBE-D3). (Color scheme: red – oxygen, blue – titanium, brown – carbon, white – hydrogen)

Figure 9. Spin-polarized frontier orbitals showing the energy gap between surface-centered and molecule-centered states. (Color scheme: red – oxygen, blue – titanium, brown – carbon, white – hydrogen)

Figure 10. DFT spin density visualization of the formation of the Ti3+ site and the organic α-radical from two different perspectives. (Color scheme: red – oxygen, blue – titanium, brown – carbon, white – hydrogen)
